title: The Great Pyramid of Giza rises under Khufu
summary: On the Giza plateau overlooking the west bank of the Nile, the Fourth-Dynasty pharaoh Khufu had raised for himself, in roughly two decades of concerted state effort, a limestone mountain 146.6 metres tall and 230 metres square at the base. Completed around 2560 BCE, the Great Pyramid was the tallest structure ever built by human beings and would remain so for more than three thousand years. It is the only surviving Wonder of the ancient world and the most instantly recognisable statement of pharaonic power ever made.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
The Fourth Dynasty of Egypt, conventionally dated from about 2613 to 2494 BCE, inherited a unified state that already had five centuries of pharaonic tradition behind it. Its founder, Sneferu, was a restlessly experimental builder: he raised the Meidum pyramid (which partially collapsed), the Bent Pyramid at Dahshur (whose designers changed the angle midway up), and the Red Pyramid at Dahshur, the first true smooth-sided pyramid in history. By the time his son Khufu came to the throne, probably in the middle of the twenty-sixth century BCE, Egyptian architects understood the geometry of the pyramid and had solved most of the engineering problems that had defeated their predecessors.

Khufu (known to the Greeks as Cheops) is a curiously shadowy figure. Egyptologists place his reign somewhere between about 2589 and 2566 BCE on the conventional chronology, though a slightly earlier bracket of roughly 2609 to 2584 BCE is also defended. He was the son of Sneferu and Queen Hetepheres I, whose remarkable gilded furniture was recovered from a shaft tomb near his pyramid in 1925. His own contemporary image survives in only one certain sculpture, an ivory statuette barely seven centimetres tall discovered by Flinders Petrie at Abydos in 1903 and now in the Egyptian Museum in Cairo. Everything else, the towering reputation, the tyrannical persona described by Herodotus in the fifth century BCE, the tall tales of enslaved multitudes, is either later gossip or nineteenth-century invention.

Khufu chose as the site of his tomb a limestone escarpment at Giza, then a virgin plateau on the desert edge some 15 kilometres upstream of the old capital at Memphis. The plateau's local limestone provided most of the pyramid's core; finer white Tura limestone for the outer casing was quarried across the Nile and shipped in on canal barges; the granite for the internal chambers came from Aswan, more than 800 kilometres to the south. The scale of the logistics prefigured the empires of the future: a bureaucracy that could feed, clothe, and organise tens of thousands of workers for a generation.

image.caption: The ivory statuette of Khufu from Abydos, the only certain contemporary portrait of the pharaoh, Egyptian Museum, Cairo.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
The construction of the Great Pyramid, which the Egyptians themselves called Akhet Khufu, the Horizon of Khufu, occupied most of Khufu's reign, conventionally about 20 years and possibly as many as 26. The finished monument stood 146.6 metres (481 feet) tall, with each of its four sides measuring 230.33 metres at the base and aligned within a small fraction of a degree of true north. Its faces sloped inward at roughly 51 degrees 50 minutes. Modern estimates count some 2.3 million individual blocks in the structure, weighing on average 2.5 tonnes each and totalling around 5.5 million tonnes of limestone, 8,000 tonnes of granite, and 500,000 tonnes of gypsum mortar.

The architect of record was Hemiunu, Khufu's vizier and nephew, whose seated limestone statue was recovered from his mastaba tomb at Giza in 1912 and now sits in the Roemer- und Pelizaeus-Museum in Hildesheim. He commanded a permanent workforce that recent excavation of the pyramid workers' town by Mark Lehner and Zahi Hawass, southeast of the pyramid at Heit el-Ghurab, has shown was housed, fed a diet heavy in beef and fish, and organised into named crews. The best current estimate, based on ration accounts and settlement size, is a permanent labour force of about 13,000 to 20,000, swelling seasonally to perhaps 40,000. These were free Egyptians, not slaves; the classical image of whip-driven bondsmen is nowhere supported by the archaeology.

A remarkable direct testimony surfaced in 2013 when a French-Egyptian team led by Pierre Tallet excavated a set of storage galleries at Wadi al-Jarf on the Red Sea coast and recovered the papyri of a middle-ranking official named Merer. The Merer papyri, written in hieratic during the twenty-seventh year of Khufu's reign (probably very near the end of the pyramid project), record Merer's diary of transporting white Tura limestone by boat across the Nile to the port at the foot of the Giza plateau. They are the oldest inscribed papyri ever found, and they place a working named human being at the pyramid on datable days.

Inside the monument three main chambers were cut or built: an unfinished chamber deep in the bedrock, a so-called Queen's Chamber (a modern misnomer) in the middle of the mass, and the King's Chamber, roofed with nine enormous granite beams and reached through the corbelled Grand Gallery, one of the most audacious pieces of stone engineering in antiquity. A polished red granite sarcophagus, empty when the tomb was first entered in the medieval period, still sits in the King's Chamber. The original entrance was concealed behind casing stones and offset from the true centreline. Above the King's Chamber, muon-imaging surveys by the ScanPyramids project in 2017 and 2023 have revealed at least one previously unknown void, roughly 30 metres long, whose function is still debated.

image.caption: The Great Pyramid of Khufu at Giza, its outer casing long stripped to reveal the limestone core.
image.credit: Nina Aldin Thune, via Wikimedia Commons

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
Khufu was buried in his pyramid around 2560 BCE. His two sons, Djedefre and then Khafre, succeeded him. Djedefre built his pyramid a few kilometres north at Abu Rawash, but Khafre returned to the Giza plateau and raised the second and slightly smaller pyramid immediately south of his father's, together with the Great Sphinx and the extraordinary Valley Temple of red granite and alabaster. Khafre's son Menkaure completed the Giza group with the third and smallest of the three main pyramids. Together the family had built, within about seventy-five years, the largest coherent complex of monumental architecture the world had yet seen.

The strain, however, was considerable. No later Egyptian dynasty ever again mustered the resources to build on the scale of the Great Pyramid. The Fifth-Dynasty pharaohs shifted to smaller pyramids at Abusir and to elaborate sun temples; the Sixth Dynasty ended in what looks archaeologically like a slow-motion collapse of central authority, followed by the political fragmentation of the First Intermediate Period. Whether the pyramid-building economy was itself responsible for the decline, as some historians have argued, or whether climate change (a Nile-flood shortfall around 2200 BCE is well attested in lake cores) played the decisive role, remains debated.

The monument itself was broken into repeatedly in antiquity. Its polished white limestone casing was stripped in the medieval period, most systematically after the Cairo earthquake of 1303, to build the mosques, madrasas, and city walls of Fatimid and Mamluk Cairo. The pyramid we see today is the stepped inner core; only a small patch of casing survives at the very base of the north face. Even so, the structure remained the tallest building in the world until the completion of Lincoln Cathedral's spire in England around 1311 CE, a run of nearly 3,900 years.

image.caption: The three pyramids of Khufu, Khafre, and Menkaure at Giza, seen from the south.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
For the classical world the Great Pyramid was already a wonder. Herodotus visited Giza in the mid-fifth century BCE and left an entertaining and largely fictional account, in which 100,000 slaves toil for twenty years under a cruel king. Diodorus Siculus and Strabo repeated the story, and the pyramid duly appeared on every list of the seven wonders of the ancient world; it is the only one still standing. Napoleon's savants, who accompanied the French invasion of Egypt in 1798, made the first modern surveys, and the pyramid became a talismanic image of European Egyptomania that shaped architecture from Freemason halls to the Louvre's glass pyramid to the Luxor casino in Las Vegas.

Scientifically the monument has been a laboratory since Colonel Howard Vyse blasted his way into the upper chambers in 1837 (and, notoriously, into the relieving chambers above the King's Chamber, where he found the only inscribed cartouche of Khufu preserved anywhere in the pyramid). Petrie's careful survey of 1880 to 1882 established modern measurements; Reisner and Junker mapped the surrounding mastaba fields; Lehner and Hawass have transformed our picture of the workers' town. Since 2015 the ScanPyramids muon-tomography collaboration has probed the interior without cutting a single stone.

But the deepest legacy is symbolic. The Great Pyramid is what a durable, literate, agrarian state can do when it decides to defy time on behalf of a single human being. Every subsequent monumental tomb, from the ziggurat of Ur to the Mausoleum of Halicarnassus to the Taj Mahal to Lenin's tomb in Red Square, sits somewhere in its long shadow.

worldContext.text: In Sumer the Early Dynastic period was well advanced. Rival city-states such as Ur, Uruk, Lagash, Nippur, and Kish fought and traded with one another under kings whose names are preserved in the Sumerian King List. The Royal Cemetery of Ur, whose spectacular gold and lapis grave goods Leonard Woolley would excavate in the 1920s, dates from roughly this period. Cuneiform, invented at Uruk seven centuries earlier, was by now recording not just accounts but hymns, contracts, and dynastic propaganda.

In the Indus valley the mature Harappan civilisation was taking shape. The great planned cities of Mohenjo-daro and Harappa, with their gridded streets, standardised baked bricks, and centralised granaries, would reach their peak between about 2600 and 1900 BCE. Uniform weights, a still-undeciphered seal script, and long-distance trade in carnelian and lapis linked the Indus valley to Mesopotamia, where Sumerian scribes referred to a distant land called Meluhha almost certainly meaning the Indus.

In north China the late Longshan cultures along the Yellow River were consolidating into what Chinese archaeologists identify with the semi-legendary Xia dynasty. At Taosi in southern Shanxi a walled town of some 280 hectares included an elite quarter, a large cemetery with graded tombs, and what may be an astronomical observatory. Along the middle Yangtze, jade-working traditions descended from Liangzhu continued, and rammed-earth city walls appeared at a number of sites.

On Salisbury Plain in southern England the great sarsen circle and trilithons of Stonehenge were being erected in this century, replacing the earlier timber and bluestone arrangements. Across northern and western Europe the last of the Neolithic megalith-builders were still active, but the Bell Beaker complex, which would spread copper metallurgy, individual burial, and a distinctive pottery style from the Atlantic to Central Europe, was on the verge of transforming the continent.

In the Supe valley of coastal Peru the Norte Chico or Caral civilisation was at or just past its peak. Monumental platform mounds up to 20 metres tall, sunken circular plazas, and residential quarters at Caral, Aspero, and half a dozen sister sites represent the earliest complex society in the Americas. Farther north in Mesoamerica the archaic period continued; maize agriculture was spreading, but the Olmec would not appear as a distinct culture for another 1,300 years.

In highland New Guinea the intensive horticulture attested at Kuk Swamp continued to spread, with taro, banana, and yam cultivated in drained wetland gardens whose earliest examples date back more than seven thousand years. Australia was inhabited by hunter-gatherer peoples whose art traditions, including the Bradshaw or Gwion Gwion figures of the Kimberley, may already have been ancient. Remote Oceania beyond the Solomons was still unpopulated by humans.

figures.name: Khufu (Cheops)
figures.role: Second pharaoh of Egypt's Fourth Dynasty and builder of the Great Pyramid; reigned c. 2589 to 2566 BCE
figures.credit: Ivory statuette from Abydos, Egyptian Museum Cairo; photo Jon Bodsworth, via Wikimedia Commons

figures.name: Hemiunu
figures.role: Vizier and probable chief architect of the Great Pyramid; nephew of Khufu
figures.credit: Statue in the Roemer- und Pelizaeus-Museum, Hildesheim, via Wikimedia Commons

figures.name: Merer
figures.role: Middle-ranking Fourth-Dynasty official whose logbooks, found at Wadi al-Jarf in 2013, record the shipment of Tura limestone to the pyramid site

figures.name: Sneferu
figures.role: Father of Khufu and founder of the Fourth Dynasty, whose experimental pyramids at Meidum and Dahshur perfected the true-pyramid form

figures.name: Hetepheres I
figures.role: Mother of Khufu, whose gilded funerary furniture was discovered in a shaft near the Great Pyramid in 1925

images.caption: The Great Pyramid of Giza seen from the northeast, its outer casing long stripped away to reveal the stepped limestone core.
images.credit: Nina Aldin Thune, via Wikimedia Commons
